1: The angelic guide brings Ezekiel to the gate facing east. The east gate — the direction of the rising sun, of Eden (Genesis 2:8), of the glory that had departed (Ezekiel 10:19; Ezekiel 11:23) — is now the gate of the returning presence. Matthew 24:27: 'As the lightning comes from the east and shines as far as the west, so will be the coming of the Son of Man.' The east gate is the gate of the Parousia; Ezekiel stands at the entrance of the event for which all history yearns.
2: 'And behold, the glory of the God of Israel was coming from the east. And the sound of his coming was like the sound of many waters, and the earth shone with his glory.' The returning glory from the east, with a voice like many waters — and the earth shining with his glory. In Revelation, John sees one like a Son of Man whose voice is 'like the roar of many waters' (Revelation 1:15). The same voice-description from Ezekiel's glory-vision is given to the enthroned Christ. The earth shining with his glory points to Revelation 21:23-24: 'the city has no need of sun or moon ... for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb.'
3: The vision matches the vision by the Chebar canal and the vision when he came to destroy the city. Ezekiel's visions form a unified series: Merkabah at Chebar (Ezekiel 1), judgment on Jerusalem (Ezekiel 9-10), return of the glory here (Ezekiel 43). The same divine chariot that carried the glory away now carries it back. Revelation 4-5 recasts the Merkabah vision with the Lamb at its center: the one who received the seven-sealed scroll from the Ancient of Days is the returned glory in its NT form — Christ enthroned above the four living creatures.
4: 'As the glory of the LORD entered the temple by the gate facing east...' The glory enters through the east gate, permanently consecrating it. Ezekiel 44:2 will specify that this gate is to be shut permanently because 'the LORD, the God of Israel, has entered by it.' Jesus enters Jerusalem from the east through the Golden Gate (Matthew 21:1-11); the traditional identification of this as the Messiah's entry gate is rooted in Ezekiel 43:4. The gate through which Christ entered — his incarnation, his passion, his resurrection — is uniquely his: the door marked with his return, held open for his parousia.
5: 'And the Spirit lifted me up and brought me into the inner court, and behold, the glory of the LORD filled the temple.' The Spirit carries the prophet to witness the moment of filling. John 1:14: 'And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth.' The incarnation is the new filling of the temple with glory — what the cloud did for the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34-35) and for Solomon's temple (1 Kings 8:10-11), Christ's body does permanently. The Spirit who lifts Ezekiel to witness the filling is the same Spirit who overshadowed Mary and filled the new temple at conception (Luke 1:35).
6: 'While the man was standing beside me, I heard one speaking to me out of the temple.' Two figures: the angelic guide who stands beside the prophet and the divine voice from within the filled temple. The double-figure pattern throughout this vision anticipates the incarnate mediation of Christ: visible human figure alongside the speaking divine presence. In Jesus both figures collapse into one — the man who stands beside us IS the voice that speaks from the Father's fullness (John 1:18: 'the only God, who is at the Father's side, he has made him known').
7: 'This is the place of my throne and the place of the soles of my feet, where I will dwell in the midst of the people of Israel forever.' The divine declaration: my throne, my footstool, my permanent dwelling. 'Son of man' addressed to Ezekiel is the title Jesus claims for himself from Daniel 7:13, signaling he is the one who inherits this throne. Revelation 3:21: 'The one who conquers, I will grant to sit with me on my throne.' The throne's permanent dwelling among the people is Revelation 21:3: 'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people.'
8: Israel's kings had profaned the sanctuary by placing their thresholds beside YHWH's threshold, their doorposts beside his doorposts, only a wall between them. Human royal pretension crowding the divine king's space. Christ makes no such pretension — he does not grasp at equality with God (Philippians 2:6) but empties himself, and is therefore given the Name above every name. His humility removes the wall between human and divine: he does not crowd the Father's threshold but IS the threshold ('I am the door', John 10:9), and in him the two thresholds are united in one person without confusion.
9: 'Now let them put away their whoredom and the corpses of their kings far from me, and I will dwell in their midst forever.' Permanent indwelling conditioned on idolatry's removal. Christ accomplishes the removal: 'by canceling the record of debt that stood against us with its legal demands ... he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame' (Colossians 2:14-15). The idols that blocked YHWH's permanent dwelling are stripped away at the cross. The new covenant promise ('I will never leave you nor forsake you', Hebrews 13:5) is unconditional precisely because Christ has already met the condition — the idols have been put away.
10: 'Son of man, describe to the house of Israel the temple, that they may be ashamed of their iniquities, and let them measure the plan.' The vision of the perfect sanctuary is shown to produce shame and repentance. John 12:32: 'And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.' The display of the true temple — Christ crucified — simultaneously shames human sin and draws sinners to God. Hebrews 12:2: 'looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame.' The measuring of the plan is the measuring of Christ's person and work.
11: 'And if they are ashamed of all that they have done, make known to them the plan of the temple ... that they may observe all its laws and all its statutes and carry them out.' Shame precedes instruction; repentance opens the door to knowing the design. In Christ: 'I will put my laws into their minds and write them on their hearts' (Hebrews 8:10). The shameful seeing of the cross (Ezekiel 43:10) leads to the heart-writing of the law (Ezekiel 43:11) — the exact sequence of new covenant transformation. The architectural plan becomes the new-covenant ordinances written not on walls but within.
12: 'This is the law of the temple: the whole territory on top of the mountain all around shall be most holy. Behold, this is the law of the temple.' The entire holy mountain is the most holy place. Hebrews 12:22: 'You have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem.' In Christ, the 'law of the temple' is extended from the inner chamber to the entire mountain — and in the new creation, from the mountain to the whole cosmos. 1 Corinthians 6:19-20: 'Your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit ... glorify God in your body.' The believer is the most holy zone, because Christ's Spirit dwells within.
13: The altar measurements begin: the base, the ledge, the standard cubit. The altar is the place where God and his people meet through sacrifice. In Christ, the altar is his cross. The precise architectural specifications say: this meeting was exactly planned — not improvised, not accidental. Hebrews 9:22: 'Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.' The altar's careful dimensions enforce the doctrine that atonement is structurally necessary, not optional, and Christ's cross is its perfectly measured fulfillment.
14: The lower and upper settles (ledges) of the altar — 2 cubits to the first ledge, then 4 cubits to the greater settle. The layered structure of the altar = layered access. The Mosaic sacrificial system had graduated levels of approach: Levites, priests, high priest. Christ collapses all layers into one: 'through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father' (Ephesians 2:18). The two levels of the altar — lower settle and greater settle — are unified in the one sacrifice of Christ who descends to our depth (incarnation) and ascends to the highest ledge (ascension).
15: The altar hearth (אריאל, Ariel), 4 cubits tall with 4 horns at its corners. Ariel means 'lion of God' and is also a poetic name for Jerusalem (Isaiah 29:1). Four horns at four corners = the universal reach of the sacrifice. The four altar horns were the points where blood was applied (Exodus 29:12) and where fugitives could take refuge (1 Kings 1:50): they declare both atonement and asylum. At Christ's cross-altar, blood is applied to the four corners of the cosmos and every fugitive from divine justice finds asylum: 'we have fled for refuge to lay hold on the hope set before us' (Hebrews 6:18).
16: The altar hearth is 12 cubits square. Twelve = the tribes of Israel and the apostles of Christ. The twelve-square altar encompasses the full people of God: the OT twelve tribes and the NT twelve apostles who together constitute the walls and foundations of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:12-14). The sacrifice offered on this altar is for all twelve — for the full number of the redeemed drawn from every direction of the compass.
17: The ledge is 14 cubits square, with a border of half a cubit and a base of one cubit, with steps facing east. Fourteen is twice seven — the number of completeness doubled. Matthew's genealogy organizes history into three sets of fourteen generations (3 × 14 = 42) leading to Jesus (Matthew 1:17). The fourteen-cubit ledge carries the numerological signature of the Christ who arrives at the end of the seven-fold genealogical fullness. The steps face east — ascent toward the altar is oriented toward the coming glory.
18: 'These are the ordinances for the altar: On the day when it is erected for offering burnt offerings upon it and for throwing blood against it.' The day of altar dedication — the day the altar comes into its proper use. The cross is the day of the true altar's definitive dedication: the once-for-all sacrifice that ends all prior dedications. Hebrews 10:10: 'We have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.' The day of dedication is finished; the altar is permanently consecrated; the sacrifice needs no repetition.
19: 'You shall give to the Levitical priests of the family of Zadok, who draw near to me to minister to me, a bull from the herd for a sin offering.' The Zadokite priests offer the sin-offering bull. Zadok means 'righteous.' The true Zadokite — the righteous one who ministers before the LORD — is Christ: Acts 3:14 calls him 'the Holy and Righteous One'; 1 John 2:1 names him 'Jesus Christ the righteous' as our advocate. He is from the order of Melchizedek ('king of righteousness,' Hebrews 7:2), a greater priestly house than even Zadok, and he offers not a bull but himself — the sin offering that ends all sin offerings.
20: 'You shall take some of its blood and put it on the four horns of the altar, and on the four corners of the ledge, and upon the rim all around.' Blood applied to four horns, four corners, and the entire rim. The blood of Christ reaches every corner: Revelation 7:9 sees a great multitude from 'every nation, tribe, people, and language' — the four-corner blood-application read as the four-cornered universality of the atonement. The rim of the altar is the horizon of the gospel: 'to the end of the earth' (Acts 1:8). Blood on every edge means atonement available at every extremity.
21: 'You shall also take the bull of the sin offering, and it shall be burned in the appointed place belonging to the temple, outside the sanctuary.' Burned outside the sanctuary. This is the explicit type that Hebrews identifies as fulfilled at the crucifixion: 'For the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp. So Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood' (Hebrews 13:11-12). Christ was crucified outside Jerusalem's gate — the definitive sin-offering burned outside the sanctuary, making the people holy through his blood shed in the profane space.
22: 'On the second day you shall offer a male goat without blemish for a sin offering.' The second day's sin offering. The sevenfold pattern of altar consecration continues — the ongoing purification of the altar across the full week of inauguration. While the altar must be purified daily during its seven-day dedication, Christ's single sacrifice is permanently sufficient (Hebrews 10:14: 'by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified'). The church returns daily to Christ's one sacrifice not to add to it but to receive from it.
23: 'When you have finished purifying it, you shall offer a young bull without blemish and a ram without blemish from the flock.' Completion of purification followed by the burnt offering. Hebrews 9:14: 'How much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience from dead works to serve the living God.' The without-blemish requirement is the holiness standard that only Christ meets: his offering is without spot, his blood purifies not the altar but the conscience — the deeper sacrifice achieving the deeper purification.
24: 'You shall present them before the LORD, and the priests shall throw salt on them and offer them up as a burnt offering to the LORD.' Salt on the offering — the salt of the covenant (Numbers 18:19; Leviticus 2:13). Mark 9:49-50: 'everyone will be salted with fire ... Have salt in yourselves, and be at peace with one another.' The covenant of salt is permanent and preserving. Christ's new covenant is 'a covenant of salt' — the salting of his sacrifice ratifies an everlasting bond between God and his people that neither party may dissolve.
25: 'For seven days you shall provide daily a goat for a sin offering; also a young bull and a ram from the flock, without blemish, shall be provided.' Seven days of daily sin and burnt offerings. The seven-day altar consecration mirrors the seven-day ordination of the Aaronic priests (Leviticus 8:33-36). The seven days of the Passion Week — from Palm Sunday's entry through the Sabbath rest in the tomb — are the seven days of Christ's priestly self-consecration. As the Aaronic priest spent seven days in the tent of meeting before beginning his ministry (Leviticus 8:35), Christ spent seven days of culminating obedience before completing his once-for-all sacrifice.
26: 'Seven days shall they make atonement for the altar and cleanse it, and so consecrate it.' Seven days of atonement, cleansing, and consecration — and then the altar is fully consecrated. Seven = completeness; the seven-day completion of the Passion Week ends with the Sabbath, the day of completed work. Christ 'finished the work' (John 17:4) and on the seventh day rested in the tomb — 'It is finished' (John 19:30) is the declaration that the seven days of atonement-and-consecration are complete.
27: 'And when they have completed these days, then from the eighth day onward the priests shall offer on the altar your burnt offerings and your peace offerings, and I will accept you, declares the Lord GOD.' The eighth day — the new beginning after the completed seven. The resurrection is the eighth day: Sunday is both the first day of the new creation week and the eighth day after the previous Sunday of triumphal entry, the day that breaks the cycle of the old week and inaugurates the perpetual new. 'I will accept you' — the Father's acceptance of the risen and glorified Christ is the ground of his acceptance of all who are in Christ: 'To the praise of his glorious grace, with which he has blessed us in the Beloved' (Ephesians 1:6). The peace offerings of the eighth day are Christ risen, saying 'Peace be with you' (John 20:19, 21, 26) to his priestly people gathered in the new temple.
